title: Stonk Journal Complete Guide: Setup, Features & Tips (2026)
meta_title: Stonk Journal Complete Guide: Setup, Features & Tips (2026) | TradingToolsHub
meta_description: Stonk Journal Complete Guide: Setup, Features & Tips (2026). Stonk Journal (3.9★, Free). Step-by-step setup, features walkthrough, and pro tips. Updated 2026.
excerpt: Complete guide to setting up and using Stonk Journal — from account creation to pro-level tips.
content:
What is Stonk Journal?

Stonk Journal is a completely free, ad-free trading journal designed for traders across all asset classes—stocks, options, futures, forex, and crypto. Launched in 2021 by StonkJournal, it provides a clean, straightforward platform for documenting your trades and analyzing your performance without corporate tracking, paywalls, or hidden monetization. With a solid 3.9/5 rating from users who value privacy-first tools, Stonk Journal is built for traders who want accountability and data insights without paying subscription fees. Whether you're a day trader logging swing patterns or a crypto trader tracking altcoin entries, Stonk Journal handles multiple accounts and strategies in one dashboard—and the fact that it costs $0/mo makes it accessible to beginners building their first journaling habit.

How to Create Your Stonk Journal Account

Setting up a Stonk Journal account takes fewer than 5 minutes and requires minimal information. Here's the exact process:

  • Navigate to the signup page — Visit stonkjournal.com and click "Sign Up" or "Create Free Account."
  • Enter your email address — You'll need a valid email. Stonk Journal will send a confirmation link to verify ownership.
  • Create a password — Choose a strong password with at least 8 characters. Stonk Journal doesn't have unnecessary complexity rules, so focus on something memorable but secure.
  • Confirm your email — Click the verification link in your inbox within 24 hours. Check your spam folder if the email doesn't arrive immediately.
  • Set your profile timezone — This is critical for accurate trade timestamps, especially if you trade multiple markets (US equities, forex, crypto). Stonk Journal will ask during onboarding.
  • Accept the terms — Review the privacy policy (spoiler: there's no data monetization) and agree to terms of service.
  • You're live — No credit card required, no trial period, no paywall. Your free account is immediately active.

Estimated setup time: 3–5 minutes. If you're using Stonk Journal alongside your broker, have your account details ready but don't worry—Stonk Journal doesn't directly integrate with brokers, so you won't be linking API keys or importing automatically.

Setting Up Stonk Journal for the First Time

When you first log in, you'll see the dashboard—a clean, minimal interface that avoids the overwhelming clutter of competing platforms. Here's how to configure it for your trading:

Create Your First Portfolio — Stonk Journal lets you maintain multiple portfolios for strategy segregation (e.g., "Options Wheel Strategy," "Day Trading," "HODL Portfolio"). Click "New Portfolio" and assign it a name, starting balance, and base currency. If you trade across crypto and stocks, create separate portfolios so your metrics don't cross-contaminate. This is especially useful for identifying which strategy is actually working.

Configure Account Preferences — Set your trading hours (Stonk Journal knows US market hours by default but allows customization for forex or crypto), your preferred currency for reporting, and whether you want daily or weekly performance summaries. If you daytrade, enable intraday performance tracking so you're not waiting until market close for your stats.

Link Multiple Accounts (If Needed) — If you trade from multiple brokers (interactive brokers + crypto exchange), Stonk Journal lets you add multiple accounts within the same portfolio. Name them clearly ("IB Options Account," "Kraken Spot") so you know where each trade came from when reviewing your journal later.

Enable Risk Limit Alerts — Set your daily loss limit and maximum risk-per-trade percentage in settings. Stonk Journal will warn you before you exceed these thresholds—not a hard stop, but a visible alarm that forces you to think twice.

Customize Dashboard Widgets — The dashboard is modular. Pin the widgets you care about (Win Rate, Profit Factor, Recent Trades) and hide the rest. Most traders immediately deprioritize advanced Greeks or correlation data they won't look at.

Essential Features You Should Know

1. Trade Journaling with Automatic Risk/Reward Calculation — This is Stonk Journal's core feature. Every trade entry auto-calculates your risk/reward ratio based on entry price, stop loss, and take profit target. For a $100 stock position with a $98 stop and $105 target, it instantly shows 2:1 RR—helping you filter out low-probability setups before you even enter. This eliminates manual math and keeps you disciplined.

2. Performance Analytics Dashboard — Stonk Journal displays your win rate (% of winning trades), profit factor (gross profit ÷ gross loss), average trade size, and daily/weekly/monthly returns. The analytics section is straightforward without the statistical noise that intimidates beginners. You can filter by asset class (stocks vs. options) or timeframe to see which of your strategies actually works.

3. Multi-Asset Support Across Markets — Unlike platforms that force you to choose between stocks OR options OR crypto, Stonk Journal logs all of them in one journal. You can tag trades by asset class, so your performance dashboard easily separates "I'm profitable on options spreads but underwater on cryptocurrency directional trades." This is crucial for identifying your edge.

4. Trade Setup Logging and Pattern Recognition — You can document the setup that triggered your trade entry (e.g., "breakout above 50-day MA + bullish divergence on RSI"). Over time, Stonk Journal's dashboard shows which setups have the highest win rate. This transforms your journal from a record-keeping tool into a feedback loop that refines your edge.

5. Multiple Accounts and Portfolio Segregation — Run a paper trading account and live trading account in the same platform, or separate your day-trading activity from swing-trading. Stonk Journal's portfolio system ensures your data isn't mixed, making it easy to see "I crush it on 15-minute setups but lose money on 4-hour holds."

6. Notes and Trade Context — Each trade entry has a notes field where you can document your reasoning, market conditions, or emotional state. Reviewing these months later often reveals patterns (e.g., "I break my rules when I'm frustrated after a loss" or "I miss trades during earnings because of fear of the gap-down"). This self-awareness is where journaling creates long-term edge.

7. Risk Management Tracking — Stonk Journal enforces position sizing discipline by showing your cumulative risk per day, per week, and across your account. Exceed your preset risk limit, and the platform alerts you. This feature alone prevents many traders from catastrophic 20% blowup days that come from overpositioned trades.

Stonk Journal Pricing: Which Plan Should You Choose?

Stonk Journal has exactly one pricing tier: Free. There are no premium tiers, no upsells, and no hidden costs. The entire platform—journaling, performance analytics, risk management, trade setups, multi-asset support, and multiple accounts—is available at $0/mo.

This pricing structure is unusual in a market where competing platforms often charge $10–40/month. Edgewonk, a feature-rich competitor, starts at $99/year and jumps to $299/year for advanced analytics. Trademetria offers free journaling but paywalls performance analytics at $9.99/month. TradeView Journal is completely free but has fewer integrations and less polished reporting.

Who Should Use Stonk Journal's Free Tier? Everyone. There's no limitation that scales with your account size or trading frequency. Beginner traders building their first journaling habit, intermediate traders scaling from 5 trades/day to 20 trades/day, and advanced traders managing $500K+ accounts all access the same feature set. The only constraint is manual trade entry—Stonk Journal doesn't auto-import from brokers, so high-frequency traders may find data entry time-consuming. For most traders (who place 1–30 trades per day), the time investment is 5–10 minutes per day.

Pro Tips for Getting the Most Out of Stonk Journal

1. Log Trades Immediately After Closing, Not at End of Day — The longer you wait to journal, the less accurate your emotional recollection of the setup is. Stonk Journal's notes field is worthless if you're writing from memory two hours later. Set a phone reminder to journal the moment your position closes. This habit also prevents the common mistake of misremembering entry price or risk due to confirmation bias.

2. Use Tags and Filters to Isolate Profitable Patterns — Stonk Journal lets you tag trades by setup type, time of day, or market condition. Tag all your "breakout above 50-day MA" trades, then filter your performance report to see your win rate specifically on that pattern. Most traders discover they're profitable on 1–2 setups and should just repeat those. Edgewonk does this with more automation, but Stonk Journal's manual tagging is accurate if you're disciplined.

3. Review Your Win Rate by Asset Class, Not Overall — A trader might be 60% win rate on stock options but 35% win rate on forex. If you lump them together, you get a mediocre 45% and don't realize you should abandon forex entirely. Use Stonk Journal's portfolio filtering or create separate portfolios for each asset class and review weekly. This is where most traders find their real edge—and where they identify what's burning their account.

4. Set Your Daily and Weekly Loss Limits, Then Respect Them — Stonk Journal's risk management alerts are meaningless if you ignore them and keep trading. When you hit your preset loss limit (e.g., 2% of account balance per day), close the platform. This single rule prevents a 2% loss from becoming a 10% loss after revenge trading. Most profitable traders are profitable because they cut losses early; Stonk Journal makes it visible when you're violating this rule.

5. Export and Analyze Data Monthly in a Spreadsheet — Stonk Journal's built-in analytics are solid but basic. If you want to run correlation analysis (do my stock trades perform better during high-VIX environments?) or variance calculations, export your trade data to Excel monthly. Add columns for market regime, volume spike, implied volatility, or other variables. Over 100+ trades, patterns emerge that Stonk Journal's dashboard won't surface.

6. Back Up Your Data Locally Every Quarter — Stonk Journal is reliable, but the con list mentions data loss issues reported by some users. Export your journal to CSV or PDF every 3 months as a precaution. If something goes wrong, you won't lose a year of trade history. Most journaling platforms allow bulk export; use it.

7. Use Notes Field for Emotional Meta-Data, Not Trade Mechanics — Don't write "bought at 125.50, sold at 126.10." Your trade entry already has that data. Instead, write "felt impatient, entered before signal confirmed" or "held too long hoping for +3R, should have taken +2R." After 50 trades, review your notes and you'll see patterns in *how* you're failing, not just *that* you're failing. This feedback loop is where journaling moves from record-keeping to skill development.

Common Stonk Journal Issues and How to Fix Them

Issue 1: Data Loss or Missing Trades — Some users report trades disappearing after logout or browser refresh. Fix: Stonk Journal is in active development; if you encounter this, clear your browser cache and cookies, then log back in. Make sure you're using a supported browser (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge). If data is still missing, contact support with your account email—they can often recover trades from backups. As noted above, export your data quarterly as insurance.

Issue 2: Timezone Confusion Causing Incorrect Trade Timestamps — If you set your timezone to EST but you're actually trading crypto at 2 AM UTC, your journal timestamps will be off by hours. Fix: Go to Account Settings and verify your timezone is correct. If you trade multiple markets with different sessions, consider setting your primary timezone to UTC and mentally adjusting, or create separate accounts for each timezone (e.g., "US Equities EST," "Crypto UTC").

Issue 3: Manual Entry Takes Too Long, Especially with High Trade Volume — If you're placing 50+ trades per day, manually entering each one into Stonk Journal will eat 30–45 minutes. Fix: Stonk Journal doesn't have broker integration, so you can't automate this. Options: (1) use a competing platform like TradeView Journal that auto-imports from certain brokers, (2) batch-enter trades in bulk from your broker's trade history at day's end, or (3) accept that Stonk Journal is better suited for swing traders and day traders with moderate frequency (under 20 trades/day).

Issue 4: Mobile Entry Not Possible — Stonk Journal has no mobile app, so you can't journal from your phone. Fix: Use your phone's browser to access the web version (it's mobile-responsive) and log trades between sessions, or note trade details in a phone memo and transcribe to Stonk Journal after market close. Not ideal, but workable for traders who don't need sub-minute logging.

Is Stonk Journal Worth It? Our Verdict

Stonk Journal is genuinely worth using for beginning to intermediate traders across any asset class. The combination of zero cost, clean interface, automatic risk/reward calculation, and solid performance analytics makes it the best free journaling option available in 2026. The lack of broker integration and mobile app are real limitations, but they're acceptable trade-offs for traders willing to manually log trades and review from a desktop. If you're a high-frequency trader who places 100+ daily trades or require automated broker data import, upgrade to Edgewonk ($99/year) or Trademetria ($120/year). But for anyone serious about building a journaling habit without financial friction, Stonk Journal's 3.9/5 rating and completely free model make it the fastest path to self-awareness as a trader.
author: TradingToolsHub Editorial
